sentences.text: First name or--
Both-- Sam Huber.
No.
At home, I was called Shmuel.
Shmuel-- in Hebrew or in English?
English-- S-H-M-U-E-L.
Huber-- H-U-B-E-R.
This is the Hebrew name, [INAUDIBLE] Shmuel.
The same.
That's it.
Yes.
Never.
Never.
7th of April, 1923.
Falenica.
This is a suburb of Warsaw-- more or less a suburb of Warsaw.
By train, or-- we went, usually by train.
It would be about 20 minutes.
Yeah, yes, yes, yes.
Yes.
Till-- yes, till I left when the Germans invaded.
Yes, till I left, September, '39, or October, '39.
That would be more correct.
What can I say?
My father had a shop of books.
Both secular and religious.
Yes.
The shop, the house, the home, the kitchen-- very, very congested-- very small place.
Yes, yes.
Yes, on the premises.
I had, I had an older brother-- elder brother and two sisters.
Abraham Aaron.
Golda Leah.
The youngest.
The baby at that time, yes.
The oldest was Hannah.
I couldn't remember Hannah.
The second sister was Miriam, and she was five years older than I am-- than I was.
My brother was two and 1/2 years older.
That's correct.
You'll have to be more precise in questioning me.
What shall I tell you about?
We had a very hard life.
Strictly religious.
Stricter than strict-- that should tell you anything, a fundamentalist.
I don't know sect.
Sect doesn't fit him, the word.
A Hasidic Jew, he was.
But I don't reckon this is a sect.
Yes.
Ger, or Ger, you could pronounce it, yes.
Yes.
This was a quite a well-known in Poland among the Hasidic Jews.
He was numero uno, I would say.
Very-- yes, comparatively large, yes.
Yes.
I wouldn't know much about that.
I don't remember this, so many years.
Yes.
The only-- the only interest, this, yes-- work for a livelihood and study, pray and study.
That-- yes.
Yes, yes.
I'm very hesitant to talk about it, because I would be partial, obviously.
But I did not get along with him very well.
I would say so.
Extremely strict.
Affirmative.
Very affirmative to your question.
The opposite.
The opposite.
The opposite-- very pleasant, very soft, very accommodating.

I do not remember my mother having an exchange-- I mean, being strict or having to raise her voice ever in her life-- with us, with neighbors, with anybody-- never, till the last day when I saw her, so just opposite.
Well, we had no choice in that.
Yes.
Very rigid.
They were perfectly.
I don't like to say this-- doesn't fit in the word.
Rigidly adhered-- that would be the correct.
No.
She was religious, but more progressive, I would say would be the word.
If I would have to fit in one word, I will say more enlightened.
Yes.
Secular books, yes, secular newspapers, not in my memory-- you have to understand that father was very controlling.
And it would be very hard to bring in newspaper, but books, yes.
No, my father did not love books.
My, my sisters loved books.
And she, as a matter of fact, she-- my younger sister in particularly-- introduced me to reading, yes.
Oh, she was leftist in her outlook, very leftist in her outlook.
Yes, she was a communist sympathizer, I would dare say.
As it is so many years ago, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wouldn't be sure if that is so.
I know that she was leftist, very leftist in her outlook.
But you have to realize that Jewish people in Poland were so oppressed that to be leftist was very easy, you know, just to look for something that-- for redemption, so to say.
So she was politically minded, and I would say leftist, at least.
Both sisters.
Both were, yes.
Both were like this, yes.
No, none whatsoever.
No, of course not.
Yes, yes.
Yes-- not secular books, but books for school, you know?
In Poland, you didn't get, like here in school, you got all the books.
In the shop, you sold the books that were required for school opening during the year.
So these were the books.
I don't know if you can call it secular-- for school, you know?
Textbooks, that is correct, and writing materials as well.
No, both.
Other--
Other than that, other than that were Hebrew books.
No.
They-- when I was born, I don't think they were alive anymore.
Yes.
Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.
No, none of them, none of them.
From my mother's side, some were living in Warsaw proper.
And some were living in a city called Opoczno in Poland.
That would be about two hours, I would say, travel by train-- two to three hours travel by train from Warsaw.
No, not frequently, only on occasions.
In Warsaw, where I learned, where I studied from the age of 12 till 14, I was a boarder in the school.
So I used to go to my mother's sister.
Yes, a Saturday afternoon, you know, for a visit-- or i used to go to my father's brother, who lived in the center of Warsaw as well.
Other family lived further away with no money to go.
No, wasn't--
Very limited.
Unfortunate, not secular whatsoever.
No, no, none whatsoever.
What I know is just self-acquired.
At the age of three.
Cheder.
At the age of three, yes-- sound, sounds strange, no?
Yes.
Yeah.
From the morning till late in the night.
This I wouldn't say.
This I don't remember, but later, at the age of seven, eight, definitely.

We used to come home-- I used to come home 3 o'clock or 2:30 to, to have-- I don't know what you call it, would be lunch or dinner-- and go back to study and come back late in the night-- 9 o'clock, after 8.
This was the day's study.
Very hard-- what should I tell you what I learned?
Subjects, we had-- the subjects-- there's no such thing in, in Jewish schooling as finishing school.
You know, you can learn all your life and know just a little bit.
I learned religious studies.
That's all.
It's very hard.
Oh, no read and write Hebrew didn't-- doesn't come into this.
No, it's an entirely different field.

As a matter of fact, just the opposite-- in, in religious, strict religious studies, you just ignore modern Hebrew or modern language, Hebrew language, as that was taboo, just religious studies, which was mostly Aramaic-- old Hebrew and Aramaic.
Talmud, Talmud, yes.
Torah, Talmud, yes.
Age of three and continued progressively advanced, more and more, yes.
No coeducational schools in our home.
I don't remember any names.
To begin, the cheder was in a private home-- very poor.
There was no electricity, no water, no timber floor.
It had a kerosene lamp-- absolutely primitive.
And I try to remember now, it's like, I would say, in Third World countries now.
Heat, I don't remember so much.
But cold, definitely, because heating was a problem.
Yes-- not a desk.
I don't know what it was.
Some kind of improvised.
Table, chair, yes.
I would say in the Cheder would be 12 to 15.
Not to my knowledge, [LAUGHTER] no, no, I wouldn't say so.
It was obligatory, and that's it.
You accept.
I have learned from the very beginning to accept things that cannot be resisted.
That was the way it was.
I had no choice in that.
Democracy didn't exist.
The word democracy, I learned much later in my life-- choice, democracy, no.
Oh, the garments, that was-- how can I describe it?
Dark, drab-- it was a ghetto clothing, you know, that it was imposed on Jews hundreds of years ago.
So later, they accepted it on their own volition.
From-- that is, that is correct.
That is correct.
Or [? wasn't ?] religious-- there were some Jews who didn't wear that, that wore modern clothes.
There were, of course, quite a number.
Then it was obligatory.
You couldn't have any other way.
Yes-- sidelocks, you mean.
Absolutely, yes.
That was a problem, by the way.
Walking in Warsaw with sidelocks was courting danger.
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say in Warsaw, while I was studying in yeshiva, whenever I went out in the streets, I was kicked or abused-- verbally abused, assaulted.
More or less-- the exception would be to be in a tolerant surrounding.
Yes, kicked.
It would be just-- not bashed up is a bit more, but--
Pushed, kicked, yes.
I would, I would say so.
I would say that Aborigines here have got a very good life in comparison with our life, yes.
And besides, in Warsaw there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Jewish part, the part the Jews lived, our life was a ghetto life.
You were much safer.
Yes, but once you went out further, you know, when I, when I went to my parents' home and I had to go to the train, then when danger starts.
That was-- yes, yes, yes.
No, no, no, no, no such thing.
You are talking about things that didn't exist.
Nobody.
This-- oh, yeah, that how it was.
This is how life was.
I remember I was always living in fear, yes.
I remember I always fearful, looking back here, there.
What is happening?
Who is coming?
Yes, yes.
I don't know three.
I don't remember three.
I-- my memory begins, I would say-- it's starting to crystallize at the age of five.
Yes.
Yes, most definitely.
Yes.
I remember, interesting thing, one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, I would, I would go back.
We had neighbors who were more tolerant.
We were neighbors-- we had neighbors who were not so bad.
But particular, I remember one who was a schoolteacher.
He used to come to buy books for the school.
And he-- I always look at him like some kind of, like a Polish angel.
Because he was very gentle, very pleasant and polite.
This is the exception, I remember.
He was outstanding, because he was different.
Very nice, oh, he was very nice.
But I was-- again, so we had neighbors who were more tolerant.
They knew us, so-- attacked-- you were always attacked by a stranger, not by a neighbor.
Neighbors had a certain tolerance.
I would say it was not an enforced segregation.
But it was-- different parts of the city were Jewish and different parts were Polish.
Oh, mixed-- yeah, yeah, yeah, yes, yes.
Yes.
Not that I remember.
Separate lives.
Different language, different culture.
Yiddish.
Yes, I understand Polish.
Even now, I understand Polish.
But I spoke Polish very hesitantly.
I never learned it.
It was never my language.
It depends what you say.
In, in today's terms, I would say definitely, yes.
Very hard living, very hard living-- we could never afford what we wanted, never.
Even in food, we were limited to what we could afford and what we couldn't afford.
Enough, yes, but a very restricted diet-- bread, potatoes, not much meat, cake once a week.
Cake-- Shabbat.
Only Shabbat.
Got a slice, and that's it.
The cheapest.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
But ask me about butter or coffee or tea-- here came in the restrictions.
Tea, you used to rinse the same-- we used to buy tea in 10-gram bags.
You used to rinse and rinse, since the color was colorless.
Yes.
Every drop, and more than that.
And coffee, used-- we couldn't afford coffee.
So we bought a product called chicory.
I don't-- [INAUDIBLE] I think you have it here in, in Australia-- that gives color, but tastes different.
Just people should think we have coffee.
Yes, every Sunday was observed holiday, because it was state-enforced, every Saturday because we were Jewish.
No, Saturday was strict.
Sunday was official.
And unofficially, you know, then you observed it much less.
[INAUDIBLE] 16 days a year-- eight days Pesach, Passover, and eight days in Sukkot.
That's it.
No learning.
While I was young, I would say at least twice a day.
Morning and evening, yes.
Spend half a day, yes.
All day Yom Kippur and most of the day on Rosh Hashanah.
No, there was other holidays, if you know.
There's Shavuot.
Jewish holidays.
Yes.
Was interrupted, yes, yes.
I do not imagine, because life was very hard.
Financially, it was-- we were very-- how should I say?
Insecure, can I say so?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To meet a payment was always a problem.
Yeah, of course I was aware, of course.
We couldn't afford anything.
An item of new clothes was always an event.
Not particularly, no.
My mother had a brother who lived in Opoczno, as I said.
And he was very well-off.
And he used, from time to time, Opoczno to help us.
Occasionally, yes, would send money.
Not every-- that's not every day.
Till the age of 12.
I was sent to Warsaw to-- for exam.
Different to what you see today-- the exam was that the principal called you in.
And he said, well, what do you know about this item or that item, he asked.
And if he liked, if you answered very satisfactory, it means [INAUDIBLE].
And I was at the 12-year-- I was then 12 years old.
That was in 1935.
And I won't-- it sounds a bit boasting, but was a very gifted.
They decided I was--
Yes, and I went to yeshiva in Warsaw then.
Yes, mesivta.
No, mesivta, just mesivta.
Mesivta is Aramiac for yeshiva.
Yes, in Warsaw, yes, very much so-- even in Poland, not just in Warsaw.
Yes.
I would say in the, in the hundreds.
Yes.
Yes.
This was, by the way, located in a place where the Warsaw Uprising, the last, in 1943, was in this street.
The ghetto uprising-- the ghetto was much larger, because there were half a million Jews at that time in the ghetto.
But this was the last stand.
At the place-- not-- the street.
That place was-- the Germans employed there-- I think brush, they manufactured brushes there, I think.
I'm not sure.
Anyway, this was the place where the Jewish proletariat, can I say so?
Prolets, ?] proletariat-- the left wing of the ALP, in modern terms, existed there.
And there was--
Yes.
Thank you.
